fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The Pfennig recipe is a method for constructing bidirectional type systems that satisfy many of the criteria that are desirable for such a system. It functions as a good starting point for building up a bidirectional type system. The disadvantages of the Pfennig recipe—especially with respect to annotation discipline—can be mitigated in exchange for a larger set of rules.

Some preliminary definitions are in order.
288type-systems-000Jtype-systems-000J.xmlIntroduction rule
An introduction rule introduces a type connective that occurs in the conclusions of a rule. 289type-systems-000Ktype-systems-000K.xmlElimiation rule
An elimination rule eliminates a type connective that occurs in a premise of a rule.

The first step is to locate the principal judgement.
290type-systems-000Rtype-systems-000R.xmlPrincipal connective
The principal connective of
an introduction rule is the connective that is being introduced by the rule
an elimination rule is the connective that is being eliminated 291type-systems-000Stype-systems-000S.xmlPrincipal judgement
The principal judgement of a rule is the judgement containing the principle connective.
In an introduction rule, the principle judgement is usually the conclusion.
In an elimination rule, the principle judgement is usually the first premise.

The second step is to bidirectionalize the principal judgement.
If the rule is an introduction rule, then make the principal judgement a checking judgement.
If the rule is an elimination rule, then make the principal judgement a synthesis judgement.

The third step is to bidirectionalize the remaining judgements in the rule. The direction or mode of the principal judgment provides guidance for the direction of the other judgements.
If the principal judgement is a conclusion, then the only judgements left are premises. The conclusion's mode provides sufficient information to determine the modes of the premises. Here, we should utilize known information. For example, if a conclusion checks against a type A and a premise also contains type A, then the premise should also be a checking judgement.
If the principal judgement is a premise (usually the first), then it is better to bidrectionalize the premises first, before the conclusion. This maximizes the chances of having enough information to synthesize the type of the conclusion. As before, we should utilize known information. If we know the type of a judgement, then the judgement should use checking. Here, if an earlier premise synthesizes a type B, then a later premise having type B should use checking.
The modes we choose should be mode-correct and should also be guided by considerations of annotatability and annotation character.

Here is an example using product introduction rule (\mathtt {\times I}).
\frac {\Gamma \vdash e_1 : A_1 \quad \Gamma \vdash e_2 : A_2} {\mathbf {\Gamma \vdash \langle e_1, e_2 \rangle : A_1 \times A_2}}(\mathtt {\times I})
The principal judgment is the conclusion (in bold). Because this is an introduction rule, we make the conclusion a checking judgement.
\mathbf {\Gamma \vdash \langle e_1, e_2 \rangle \Leftarrow A_1 \times A_2}
The types A_1 and A_2 are known, so we can use checking for the premises as well.
\begin {array}{cc} \Gamma \vdash e_1 \Leftarrow A_1 & \Gamma \vdash e_2 \Leftarrow A_2 \\ \end {array}
The final bidirectionalized rule is
\frac {\Gamma \vdash e_1 \Leftarrow A_1 \quad \Gamma \vdash e_2 \Leftarrow A_2} {\mathbf {\Gamma \vdash \langle e_1, e_2 \rangle \Leftarrow A_1 \times A_2}}(\mathtt {\Leftarrow \times I})
Typically, the type assignment rules are treated as the "ground truth" against annotatability or completeness is measured. However, there are cases where these rules must be examined more critically.

Consider the following sum-elimination rule:
\frac {\begin {array}{cc} & \Gamma , x_1:A_1 \vdash e_1 : B \\ \Gamma \vdash e : A_1 + A_2 & \Gamma , x_2:A_2 \vdash e_2 : B \end {array}} {\Gamma \vdash \mathtt {case}\ e\ \mathtt {of}\ \{\mathtt {inj}_1\ x_1 \mapsto e_2\ |\ \mathtt {inj}_2\ x_2 \mapsto e_3\} : B}(\mathtt {+E})
This rule is a special case of reasoning by cases:
292ttfp-000Ottfp-000O.xmlDisjunction elimination\frac {\begin {array}{ccc} & [A] & [B] \\ & \vdots & \vdots \\ A \lor B & C & C \end {array}} {C}(\mathtt {\lor {E}})

A bidirectional system that required an annotation on every subterm would satisfy completeness, but it would be impractical to use. For this reason, a system should exemplify good annotation character, which can be defined as follows:
Annotations should be lightweight. They should constitutes a small portion of the program text.
Annotations should be predictable. There should be a clear annotation discipline that programmers can follow to determine where annotations are required.
Annotations should be stable. A small change to a program should have a small effect on the annotation requirements.
Annotations should be legible. They should be easy to read and understand.

Some hypotheses to clarify what good annotation character might entail:
A discipline that needs only local information is preferable to one that needs global information.
A discpline that requires all non-obvious type annoations and no obvious type annotations is preferable.
A discipline that needs obvious type annotations in certain situations is acceptable, if those situations are rare.

It is preferable to be able to detemine whether an annotation is required by a subterm by looking only at the neighborhood of the subterm and not the whole program. Toward that end, the Pfennig recipe leads to an annotation discipline that requires only the syntactic form of the subterm and its immediate context to determine whether the subterm requires an annotation. (Consider the difference between (\lambda x. e)\ e' and f(\lambda x. e). The first \lambda x. e requires a type annotation, while the second does not.) This locality reduces the effect of a change in another part of a program on the annotation requirements of a subterm, thus making it stable.

A bidirectional system that required an annotation on every subterm would satisfy completeness, but it would be impractical to use. For this reason, a system should exemplify good annotation character, which can be defined as follows:
Annotations should be lightweight. They should constitutes a small portion of the program text.
Annotations should be predictable. There should be a clear annotation discipline that programmers can follow to determine where annotations are required.
Annotations should be stable. A small change to a program should have a small effect on the annotation requirements.
Annotations should be legible. They should be easy to read and understand.

Some hypotheses to clarify what good annotation character might entail:
A discipline that needs only local information is preferable to one that needs global information.
A discpline that requires all non-obvious type annoations and no obvious type annotations is preferable.
A discipline that needs obvious type annotations in certain situations is acceptable, if those situations are rare.

It is preferable to be able to detemine whether an annotation is required by a subterm by looking only at the neighborhood of the subterm and not the whole program. Toward that end, the Pfennig recipe leads to an annotation discipline that requires only the syntactic form of the subterm and its immediate context to determine whether the subterm requires an annotation. (Consider the difference between (\lambda x. e)\ e' and f(\lambda x. e). The first \lambda x. e requires a type annotation, while the second does not.) This locality reduces the effect of a change in another part of a program on the annotation requirements of a subterm, thus making it stable.

A bidirectional system is complete with respect to a type assignment system if every rule in the type assignment system has a corresponding rule in the bidirectional system. In general, this correspondence is easy to achieve if we begin with a type assignment system and "bidirectionalize" each of its rules.

fr:title.text: Criteria for selecting rules for a bidirectional type system

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Criteria for selecting rules for a bidirectional type system

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
Selecting rules for a bidirectional type system affords a certain degree of freedom. One must decide whether a particular premise or conclusion synthesizes or checks a type. It is impractical to cover all possible combinations. Consider the following \rightarrow E \Rightarrow rule:
\frac {\begin {array}{cc} \Gamma \vdash e_1 : A \rightarrow B & \Gamma \vdash e_2 : A \end {array}} {\Gamma \vdash e_1 e_2 : B}

Because there are two judgement modes—synthesis and checking—and three typing judgements—\Gamma \vdash e_1 : A \rightarrow B, \Gamma \vdash e_2 : A and \Gamma \vdash e_1 e_2 : B—each rule would have 2^3=8 variants. Reasoning about such a type system would be difficult. Furthremore, having more than one variant introduces nondeterminism into the type system. One would need to guess or bruteforce or otherwise somehow determine which variant to apply. The performance cost could be significant.
This raises the question of what criteria the designer should use to select these rules.
A good rule avoids guessing types. A synthesized type should be derived from known information and not from, e.g., an enumeration of types.

A rule is mode-correct if there is a strategy for recursively deriving the premises such that two conditions hold:
The premises are mode-correct. For each premise every input meta-variable is known, both from the inputs to the rule's conclusions annd from the outputs of earlier premises.
The conclusions are mode-correct. If all premises have been derived, then the outputs of the rule's conclusions are known.

We can rewrite the last rule to reflect the effective strategy:
\frac {\begin {array}{cc} \Gamma \vdash e_2 \Rightarrow A & \Gamma \vdash e_1 \Leftarrow A \rightarrow B \end {array}} {\Gamma \vdash e_1 e_2 \Leftarrow B}

Mode-correctness is necessary for directly implementing a bidirectional type system that is deterministic. However, mode-correctness alone is insufficient for a type system to be syntax-directed. For that, the inputs (the context, the term and if checking, the type) must match the conclusion of only one rule.

A bidirectional system is complete with respect to a type assignment system if every rule in the type assignment system has a corresponding rule in the bidirectional system. In general, this correspondence is easy to achieve if we begin with a type assignment system and "bidirectionalize" each of its rules.

While the number of rules in a type system is not a strict measure of its complexity, it can be an effective heuristic for comparing two system in an inclusion relationship, e.g., when system S_1 is a strict subset of system S_2. In such cases, one can reasonably claim that S_1 is simpler than S_2, because it has fewer rules. Smaller systems tend to be easier to work with.

A bidirectional system that required an annotation on every subterm would satisfy completeness, but it would be impractical to use. For this reason, a system should exemplify good annotation character, which can be defined as follows:
Annotations should be lightweight. They should constitutes a small portion of the program text.
Annotations should be predictable. There should be a clear annotation discipline that programmers can follow to determine where annotations are required.
Annotations should be stable. A small change to a program should have a small effect on the annotation requirements.
Annotations should be legible. They should be easy to read and understand.

Some hypotheses to clarify what good annotation character might entail:
A discipline that needs only local information is preferable to one that needs global information.
A discpline that requires all non-obvious type annoations and no obvious type annotations is preferable.
A discipline that needs obvious type annotations in certain situations is acceptable, if those situations are rare.

It is preferable to be able to detemine whether an annotation is required by a subterm by looking only at the neighborhood of the subterm and not the whole program. Toward that end, the Pfennig recipe leads to an annotation discipline that requires only the syntactic form of the subterm and its immediate context to determine whether the subterm requires an annotation. (Consider the difference between (\lambda x. e)\ e' and f(\lambda x. e). The first \lambda x. e requires a type annotation, while the second does not.) This locality reduces the effect of a change in another part of a program on the annotation requirements of a subterm, thus making it stable.
An elimination rule eliminates a type connective that occurs in a premise of a rule.
An introduction rule introduces a type connective that occurs in the conclusions of a rule.
A good rule avoids guessing types. A synthesized type should be derived from known information and not from, e.g., an enumeration of types.

A rule is mode-correct if there is a strategy for recursively deriving the premises such that two conditions hold:
The premises are mode-correct. For each premise every input meta-variable is known, both from the inputs to the rule's conclusions annd from the outputs of earlier premises.
The conclusions are mode-correct. If all premises have been derived, then the outputs of the rule's conclusions are known.

We can rewrite the last rule to reflect the effective strategy:
\frac {\begin {array}{cc} \Gamma \vdash e_2 \Rightarrow A & \Gamma \vdash e_1 \Leftarrow A \rightarrow B \end {array}} {\Gamma \vdash e_1 e_2 \Leftarrow B}

Mode-correctness is necessary for directly implementing a bidirectional type system that is deterministic. However, mode-correctness alone is insufficient for a type system to be syntax-directed. For that, the inputs (the context, the term and if checking, the type) must match the conclusion of only one rule.

While the number of rules in a type system is not a strict measure of its complexity, it can be an effective heuristic for comparing two system in an inclusion relationship, e.g., when system S_1 is a strict subset of system S_2. In such cases, one can reasonably claim that S_1 is simpler than S_2, because it has fewer rules. Smaller systems tend to be easier to work with.
